story:
Sir Deucalion climbed the western stairs to the cathedral’s great portal and looked up at the tympanum where the Lord judged the living and the dead. He had seen no one but an old mystic on his way down the mountain. Kneeling between the stone kings carved into the doorway, he took off his spurs.

Inside, the narthex lay sealed and dim. He crossed into the nave; his boots rang under the high vaults, a lone candle flickering in the north transept. At the rood screen he unlocked the lattice gate and entered the choir, where angelic faces watched from the stall backs as if to ask, What will you do? He opened the organ’s console, then the central pipe doors, and set his sword and spurs on a ledge in the casing. Seated at the bench, he rested his hands on the manuals and breathed in smoke and incense over oak and stone. The cathedral seemed to draw him into itself.

He pressed a key, and the ancient mechanical action—made by Architects of a lost art—carried air to a small pipe above his head. A tremulous alto slipped into the silence like lovers embracing. He began the one piece he knew, a chorale for worship that could wear many moods. Today it sounded meditative and unstable, as if it might lead him to passages he had never heard.

Morning light struck through a ruby trefoil in the apse. Beneath it, a saint in azure lifted a star whose beams revealed a crooked path through hostile ground. Jagged rocks bit his bare feet; a stunted black bush reached for his hem; one eye showed confusion, the other terror. Around him, in a jeweled halo, crowded the saints—filling windows, nesting in spandrels, standing in niches above spent tapers. Each had holy stories, yet priests said these same figures had ordered wars, decrees, and torments. As First Knight, Deucalion was their champion on earth.

On the vigil before his knighting, his tongue had burned with prayer. When he took his vows, he had felt Heaven set a laurel crown upon him. Years later, the Order of the Sacred Heart haggled over loans and interest, traded favors between princes and priests, and when the knights rode, it was to shadowy causes in lands as fit to plunder as to protect. The holy fire turned to ash. He no longer believed in the saints, perhaps not even in their God. Yet lately, something had begun to run through the empty places in his heart, calling in wedding tones, Believe.

A door opened. Arete entered from the sacristy with candles and an altar cloth, haloed by the eastern rose’s soft pink. She was not beautiful by her race’s noble standards; she would always be called plain. But she was a future queen, and suitors were many. He was not surprised to find her tending a forgotten shrine; the humble task suited her quiet ways. He remembered how she would sometimes arrive unnoticed, and sometimes be felt by the hush she left behind.

They had met after his knighting. Sixteen and pursued by attendants, she had answered his kneeling honor with, The honor is mine, for I know you to be a noble knight. Her gaze had lifted him out of time and returned him changed. Since then, suitors clustered at her side—some his own comrades—and the realm pressed her to choose, for the king aged and ailed. There was still room, perhaps, for one more to ask.

He looked at the saints again. He would pray to every last one if it would free him from his shining mask before it hollowed and vermin gnawed his back. But no saint would save him. He must act.

Arete reappeared, paused as if to speak, then withdrew. He let the chorale fall onto its penultimate note. The sound made chalice and pyx sing, climbed mullions and tracery, soared through vault to spire, and faded among crockets and finials. A tear struck the keys. In this place, everything reached upward. Even the crucifix—the emblem of suffering—lifted like a winged thing. So must he.
